---
title: "Beginning Python for Financial Data"
subtitle: "Machine Learning in Finance · Session 1"
# Not self-contained: quarto-live's Pyodide runtime cannot resolve its worker
# from a data: URI, so embed-resources is off. Ship session_01.html together
# with session_01_files/ and SERVE OVER HTTP (double-click serve.cmd, or
# publish to a URL — see README). The live code cells do not run from file://.
format:
  live-revealjs:
    theme: [default, ../theme/mlfin.scss]
    width: 1280
    height: 720
    margin: 0.06
    center: false
    transition: fade
    transition-speed: fast
    background-transition: none
    slide-number: c/t
    controls: false
    progress: true
    hash: true
    history: false
    code-line-numbers: false
    highlight-style: github
    auto-stretch: false
engine: jupyter
pyodide:
  cell-options:
    autorun: true
    startover: true
execute:
  echo: true
  warning: false
---

# Welcome {.divider}

::: {.divider-kicker}
Machine Learning in Finance · Session 1
:::

::: {.divider-note}
Programming and machine learning for people who have never written a line of
code. We start from zero.
:::


# Who is teaching this

<div class="whoami">
<div class="who-col">
<h3>The academic part</h3>
<ul>
<li><strong>Jonas Theill Bøjstrup</strong>, PhD student</li>
<li>Research: <strong>image-based prediction in finance</strong> and deep learning</li>
<li>Teaching this course for the <strong>second time</strong></li>
<li>Supervised <strong>6+</strong> master's theses</li>
</ul>
</div>
<div class="who-col who-personal">
<h3>The rest of me</h3>
<ul>
<li>Art collector</li>
<li>Video games</li>
<li>Becoming a <strong>dad in December</strong></li>
</ul>
</div>
</div>

<div class="who-contact">Questions any time — in class, or by email at <code>jobo@econ.au.dk</code>. The second lecturer, <strong>Bezirgen Veliyev</strong>, teaches the later weeks.</div>


# Why Python?

<div class="why-grid">
<div class="why-point"><strong>Everyone uses it</strong><span>The dominant language in data science and quantitative finance. What you learn transfers to a real job.</span></div>
<div class="why-point"><strong>Batteries included</strong><span>Free libraries for data, statistics, and machine learning — pandas, scikit-learn, and many more.</span></div>
<div class="why-point"><strong>Readable</strong><span>Python code reads close to plain English. That matters enormously when you are starting out.</span></div>
<div class="why-point"><strong>Endless help</strong><span>A vast community. Almost any error you hit has been asked and answered somewhere already.</span></div>
</div>

<div class="tagline">Free, open, and runs anywhere — including, later in the course, on your own laptop with no internet.</div>


# Why learn machine learning?

Finance has become a **data discipline**.

- Asset management, risk, trading, credit, fintech — all increasingly run on
  models fit to data.
- Job postings that used to ask for "econometrics" now ask for **machine
  learning**.
- You already know one such model: **ordinary least squares**. This course
  extends that instinct to more flexible tools.

::: {.keypoint}
The goal is not to make you a computer scientist. It is to let you **read,
build, and judge** the models that finance now runs on.
:::


# What even is machine learning?

::: {.diagram}
<svg width="1120" height="440" viewBox="0 0 1120 440" role="img" aria-label="Nested circles: Artificial Intelligence contains Machine Learning, which contains Deep Learning">
  <circle cx="270" cy="220" r="205" fill="#f2f7fd" stroke="#b9d2f0" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <circle cx="270" cy="248" r="145" fill="#dcebfd" stroke="#86b6ef" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <circle cx="270" cy="290" r="72" fill="#2a78d6" stroke="#1c5cab" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <text x="270" y="52" text-anchor="middle" font-size="23" fill="#0d366b" font-weight="700">Artificial Intelligence</text>
  <text x="270" y="150" text-anchor="middle" font-size="22" fill="#16385f" font-weight="700">Machine Learning</text>
  <text x="270" y="285" text-anchor="middle" font-size="16" fill="#ffffff" font-weight="700">Deep</text>
  <text x="270" y="305" text-anchor="middle" font-size="16" fill="#ffffff" font-weight="700">Learning</text>
  <line x1="500" y1="200" x2="600" y2="200" stroke="#c3c2b7" stroke-width="1"/>
  <text x="620" y="180" font-size="19" fill="#33322f" font-weight="600">Machine learning</text>
  <text x="620" y="208" font-size="17" fill="#52514e">a model that learns patterns from</text>
  <text x="620" y="232" font-size="17" fill="#52514e">data, instead of being given</text>
  <text x="620" y="256" font-size="17" fill="#52514e">explicit rules.</text>
  <text x="620" y="300" font-size="17" fill="#898781">Deep learning is one family of ML</text>
  <text x="620" y="324" font-size="17" fill="#898781">methods; ML is one route to AI.</text>
</svg>
:::

::: {.muted .small}
Your OLS regression is already a simple machine-learning model: it *learns* its
coefficients from data. We build outward from there.
:::


# Where this course is going

<div class="timeline">
<div class="tl-row tl-now"><span class="tl-week">Week 36</span><span class="tl-topic">Programming in Python</span><span class="tl-who">JTB</span></div>
<div class="tl-row tl-now"><span class="tl-week">Week 37</span><span class="tl-topic">Foundations of machine learning</span><span class="tl-who">JTB</span></div>
<div class="tl-row tl-future"><span class="tl-week">Week 38</span><span class="tl-topic">Model selection and cross-validation</span><span class="tl-who">BV</span></div>
<div class="tl-row tl-future"><span class="tl-week">Week 39</span><span class="tl-topic">Shrinkage methods</span><span class="tl-who">BV</span></div>
<div class="tl-row tl-future"><span class="tl-week">Week 40</span><span class="tl-topic">Classification methods</span><span class="tl-who">BV</span></div>
<div class="tl-row tl-future"><span class="tl-week">Week 41</span><span class="tl-topic">Tree-based methods</span><span class="tl-who">BV</span></div>
<div class="tl-row tl-future"><span class="tl-week">Week 42</span><span class="tl-topic">Tree-based methods · course wrap-up</span><span class="tl-who">BV</span></div>
</div>

::: {.muted .small}
Teaching begins the week of **31 August 2026** (week 36). I take the first two
weeks — Python and the foundations of ML; Bezirgen takes the methods that
follow. **Later topics may still change.**
:::


# How the course works

<div class="pillars">
<div class="pillar pillar-live"><div class="pillar-tag">In class</div><div class="pillar-title">Lectures</div><div class="pillar-body">We work through concepts together, with live code you can see running. This is where ideas are introduced.</div></div>
<div class="pillar"><div class="pillar-tag">At home</div><div class="pillar-title">Exercises</div><div class="pillar-body">A notebook of short practice tasks each week, with hints and solutions folded in. This is where the learning sticks.</div></div>
<div class="pillar"><div class="pillar-tag">At home</div><div class="pillar-title">The case</div><div class="pillar-body">One financial investigation that grows across the four sessions. You carry it forward each week.</div></div>
</div>

::: {.keypoint}
Lectures are for watching and understanding. **You learn to code by doing the
exercises and the case yourself, between sessions.**
:::


# This is a demanding course

<div class="zero-to">
<div class="zt-node zt-start"><span class="zt-big">Zero</span><span class="zt-sub">no code, no ML</span></div>
<div class="zt-arrow"><span class="zt-arrow-line">———▶</span><span class="zt-arrow-label">7 weeks</span></div>
<div class="zt-node"><span class="zt-big">Python + ML</span><span class="zt-sub">foundations you can build on</span></div>
</div>

Going from **no programming** to machine learning in seven weeks is a steep
climb. Be ready for it.

- I can explain ideas and show code — but **watching is not learning to code**.
- You become fluent by **doing it yourself**. The exercises and case are where
  that happens.

::: {.keypoint}
My job is to teach the concepts and point the way. **Your job is to do the work.**
:::


# Using AI on this course

AI assistants are useful and are not banned. Use them **to learn**, not to
avoid learning.

<div class="dodont">
<div class="do-col">
<h3>Helpful</h3>
<ul>
<li>Explain an error message you do not understand</li>
<li>Explain a concept a different way</li>
<li>Suggest what a line of code does</li>
<li>Generate extra practice problems</li>
</ul>
</div>
<div class="dont-col">
<h3>Harmful</h3>
<ul>
<li>Pasting in solutions you cannot reproduce</li>
<li>Submitting code you do not understand</li>
<li>Leaning on it instead of building skill</li>
<li>Relying on it for the exam — you cannot</li>
</ul>
</div>
</div>

::: {.muted .small}
A good test: if you could not rewrite it from memory afterwards, you have not
learned it yet.
:::


# The exam

<div class="exam-facts">
<div class="exam-fact"><span class="ef-big">3 hours</span><div class="ef-label">on site, at a fixed time</div></div>
<div class="exam-fact"><span class="ef-big">No internet</span><div class="ef-label">no AI, no web search, no Colab</div></div>
<div class="exam-fact"><span class="ef-big">Your laptop</span><div class="ef-label">Python running locally</div></div>
</div>

The exam runs **on your own machine, offline**. We begin in Google Colab
because it is the easiest place to start, and later move to a local setup — a
**VS Code tutorial comes in a later session**.

::: {.keypoint}
Because the exam is offline, the skill has to be **yours**. That is the whole
reason the exercises matter.
:::


# Today

By the end of this session you can:

- run Python in a **notebook** and say what the *runtime* remembers
- store values in **variables** and use the basic **types** of data
- hold many prices in a **list** and pick out any day or window
- compute a **simple return** from two prices
- read a Python **error message** calmly

<br>

And you meet the course **case**: a four-part risk report on real US stocks.


# 1 · Notebooks {.divider}

::: {.divider-kicker}
Part 1
:::

::: {.divider-note}
Where our code lives, and how a code cell shows its result.
:::


# A notebook is a stack of cells

<div class="nb-mock">
<div class="nb-cell nb-text"><div class="nb-gutter">text</div><div class="nb-body">Compute this week's return</div></div>
<div class="nb-cell nb-code"><div class="nb-gutter">[ ]</div><div class="nb-body">(248.83 - 183.56) / 183.56</div></div>
<div class="nb-out">0.3556</div>
</div>

A **notebook** is a document read top to bottom, made of two kinds of cell. The
result of a code cell appears **directly beneath it**.


# Two kinds of cell

- **Text cells** hold explanation — headings, sentences, the story.
- **Code cells** hold Python, and can be **run**. Their output appears below.

<br>

That is the whole idea. A lecture, a calculation, and its result can live in
one scrollable document — which is exactly what these slides are.

::: {.muted .small}
We begin in **Google Colab**, which runs notebooks in your browser with
nothing to install. You need only a Google account.
:::


# Running a cell

Click a cell and press **Shift + Enter**. The cell runs and shows the value of
its **last line** — only the last one.

These slides run Python too. Run this: just one number appears.

```{pyodide}
183.56
248.83
```

::: {.muted .small}
A real Python cell, running in your browser. Change the numbers and run it
again. The same thing works identically in Colab.
:::


# Showing more than the last line

To show **several** values, use `print(...)`:

```{pyodide}
print(183.56)
print(248.83)
```

`print` is our first **function**: a named tool you use by writing its name and
round brackets. It displays whatever you put inside — each on its own line. We
will lean on it constantly.


# 2 · Python as a calculator {.divider}

::: {.divider-kicker}
Part 2
:::

::: {.divider-note}
Expressions, and leaving notes to yourself.
:::


# Expressions

An **expression** is code that produces a value. The text after a `#` is a
**comment** — Python ignores it; it is a note for humans.

```{pyodide}
120 * 45.50    # a comment: 120 shares at 45.50 each
```

Change the numbers and run it. Arithmetic works as you would expect.


# The arithmetic you have

| write | meaning |
|---|---|
| `+`  `-`  `*`  `/` | add, subtract, multiply, divide |
| `**` | power: `1.05 ** 10` is ten years of 5% growth |
| `( )` | grouping, exactly as in mathematics |

<br>

::: {.muted .small}
Order of operations is the usual one: powers, then `*` and `/`, then `+` and
`-`. Parentheses override it.
:::


# Powers: compound growth

1,000 invested at 4% for five years is a single expression:

```{pyodide}
1000 * (1 + 0.04) ** 5
```

::: {.muted .small}
Change the rate to `0.06` and run again. Compounding is just repeated
multiplication, written with `**`.
:::


# 3 · Variables {.divider}

::: {.divider-kicker}
Part 3
:::

::: {.divider-note}
Giving a value a name, so you can use it again.
:::


# Variables: naming a value

A **variable** gives a value a name. The `=` sign means **assign** — "store
this under that name" — not "equals".

```{pyodide}
price = 150.25
shares = 40
price * shares
```

::: {.diagram}
<svg width="720" height="120" viewBox="0 0 720 120" role="img" aria-label="The name price points to the value 150.25 in memory">
  <rect x="20" y="34" width="170" height="56" rx="6" fill="#eef4fc" stroke="#1c5cab" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <text x="105" y="69" text-anchor="middle" font-size="24" fill="#0d366b" font-family="Cascadia Mono, monospace">price</text>
  <line x1="195" y1="62" x2="290" y2="62" stroke="#52514e" stroke-width="2"/>
  <polygon points="290,62 277,55 277,69" fill="#52514e"/>
  <rect x="295" y="34" width="190" height="56" rx="6" fill="#ffffff" stroke="#c3c2b7" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <text x="390" y="69" text-anchor="middle" font-size="24" fill="#141413" font-family="Cascadia Mono, monospace">150.25</text>
  <text x="510" y="68" font-size="17" fill="#898781">a value held in memory</text>
</svg>
:::


# Once named, it stands for the value

Anywhere you write the name, Python uses the value:

```{pyodide}
price = 150.25
shares = 40
holding_value = price * shares
holding_value
```

::: {.muted .small}
`holding_value` now holds the result, ready to reuse — no need to retype the
multiplication.
:::


# Assignment can overwrite

Assigning to a name that already exists **replaces** its value. The old one is
gone.

Read `price = price * 1.02` from the **right**:

> take the current `price`, multiply by 1.02, and store the result back into
> `price`.


# Overwrite in action [predict]{.chip .chip-predict}

Line 2 uses `price` on **both sides** at once. What do you think that does —
and what is `price` at the end? Reason it out before you run.

```{pyodide}
#| autorun: false
price = 150.25
price = price * 1.02
price
```

::: {.muted .small}
Can a name really appear on both sides of `=`?
:::


# Naming rules

Python **requires**:

- letters, digits, and underscores only
- a name may **not start with a digit**
- no spaces: `share price` fails, `share_price` works
- case matters: `Price` and `price` are two different names


# Naming: your judgment

Both are legal. Only one will make sense to you next week:

```python
x = 0.0212
weekly_return = 0.0212
```

::: {.keypoint}
Write names for the person who reads the code later. That person is usually
**you**, a week from now.
:::


# Your turn [1]{.chip .chip-turn} {.your-turn}

You bought 120 shares at 45.50 and sold at 48.10. Complete the cell to compute
the **profit**.

```{pyodide}
#| autorun: false
shares = 120
buy_price = 45.50
sell_price = 48.10

profit = ...
profit
```

::: {.muted .small}
The result should be a little over 300.
:::


# 4 · The notebook's memory {.divider}

::: {.divider-kicker}
Part 4
:::

::: {.divider-note}
Now that you can make variables — the notebook remembers them between cells.
:::


# The runtime remembers — 1

Run this cell. It stores a value in memory under the name `portfolio_value`:

```{pyodide}
portfolio_value = 100 * 150.25
portfolio_value
```

The value is now **held in memory**. This memory is called the **runtime**.


# The runtime remembers — 2

A *later* cell can use what an earlier cell defined — without repeating it:

```{pyodide}
portfolio_value * 1.10
```

This works only because the cell above **ran first** and left
`portfolio_value` in memory.

::: {.keypoint}
A notebook is not just text on a page. It has a **memory** that fills up as you
run cells.
:::


# Order of execution matters

Cells can be run in **any order** you click them — and the runtime only knows
what it has been *told so far*.

Run a page top to bottom and all is well. Jump around, and a cell may look for
something that does not exist **yet**. The next two cells show it.


# A cell that runs too early [predict]{.chip .chip-predict}

Fresh runtime — nothing has defined `bonus` yet. **What do you think happens
when you run this?** Talk it through before you press Run.

```{pyodide}
#| autorun: false
bonus * 2
```

::: {.muted .small}
Does Python guess a value? Refuse? Something else?
:::


# Define it, then it works

Give `bonus` a value, and the very same expression works:

```{pyodide}
#| autorun: false
bonus = 1200
bonus * 2
```

The error before was not a fault in the code — the runtime simply had
**nothing stored** under that name. Define it first, and all is well.


# The safe habit

When a notebook gets confusing, reset it: **Runtime → Restart and run all**.

A notebook is only trustworthy if it runs cleanly **top to bottom from a fresh
start**. We will hold to that all course.

::: {.callout-tip appearance="simple"}
Restarting clears the runtime's memory and re-runs every cell in order. It fixes
almost every "but it worked a minute ago" mystery.
:::


# 5 · Every value has a type {.divider}

::: {.divider-kicker}
Part 5
:::

::: {.divider-note}
Numbers, text, and true/false — and why the difference costs money.
:::


# Two kinds of number

Python tells you the type of any value with the `type(...)` function:

```{pyodide}
print(type(40))
print(type(150.25))
```

- `int` — whole numbers: share counts, trading days
- `float` — numbers with a decimal point: prices, returns, rates


# Division always gives a float

Even when it divides evenly:

```{pyodide}
100 / 4
```

::: {.muted .small}
The result is `25.0`, a float — not `25`. Division in Python always produces a
decimal number.
:::


# Text: strings

A **string** is text inside quotes — tickers, names, dates:

```{pyodide}
ticker = "AAPL"
print(ticker)
print(type(ticker))
```


# Gluing strings together

`+` between strings joins them. It does **not** add:

```{pyodide}
ticker = "AAPL"
ticker + " is a US stock"
```

::: {.callout-warning appearance="simple"}
`"150"` in quotes is *text that looks like a number*. It is not a number — and
that difference causes real bugs, because prices read from files often arrive
as text.
:::


# Numbers in disguise [predict]{.chip .chip-predict}

Both values are wrapped in quotes. **What will `+` do here** — and is it what
you would want? Decide before you run.

```{pyodide}
#| autorun: false
"10" + "5"
```

::: {.muted .small}
Talk to your neighbour first.
:::


# Numbers in disguise — the fix

`float(...)` turns text into a number. Now it really adds:

```{pyodide}
float("10") + float("5")
```

::: {.keypoint}
No error is **not** the same as no mistake. `"10" + "5"` gave `"105"` with no
complaint — a silently wrong answer is the dangerous kind.
:::


# Showing results nicely

An **f-string** builds text with values dropped in. Put an `f` before the
quotes, and variables inside `{ }`:

```{pyodide}
price = 248.83
f"Apple closed at {price}"
```

::: {.muted .small}
Useful, but that number could be tidier. Next slide.
:::


# The two recipes we will reuse

A colon inside the braces sets the **format**:

```{pyodide}
price = 248.83
r = 0.0231457
print(f"Price: {price:.2f}")
print(f"Return: {r:.2%}")
```

- `:.2f` — a plain number, 2 decimals
- `:.2%` — a percentage, 2 decimals

::: {.muted .small}
These two cover almost everything we need all course.
:::


# True or False: booleans

A **comparison** asks a yes/no question. The answer is a `bool`:

```{pyodide}
price = 248.83
price > 250
```

Change `250` to `240` and run again.


# Comparison operators

| ask | write |
|---|---|
| equal? | `==`  (two signs — a single `=` *assigns*) |
| not equal? | `!=` |
| greater / less? | `>`  `<`  `>=`  `<=` |

```{pyodide}
ticker = "AAPL"
ticker == "AAPL"
```

::: {.muted .small}
Booleans look modest now. Later they select rows of data, and later still they
become the thing a model predicts.
:::


# Your turn [2]{.chip .chip-turn} {.your-turn}

This cell runs with **no error** — and gives nonsense. Run it, look closely,
then fix it so `total_cost` is a real number.

```{pyodide}
#| autorun: false
price_text = "52.40"    # arrived from a file, as text
quantity = 4

total_cost = price_text * quantity
total_cost
```

::: {.muted .small}
What is `"52.40" * 4`? Then reach for `float(...)`.
:::


# 6 · Functions and methods {.divider}

::: {.divider-kicker}
Part 6
:::

::: {.divider-note}
You have met `print` and `type`. Here are more, and the pattern behind them.
:::


# More functions

A **function** takes input in parentheses and returns a value. Two new ones:

```{pyodide}
print(round(3.14159, 2))
print(len("JPMorgan"))
```

`round` rounds to a number of decimals; `len` counts characters.


# Functions we will use constantly

| function | returns |
|---|---|
| `print(x)` | displays a value *(already using it)* |
| `type(x)` | the type of a value *(already using it)* |
| `round(x, n)` | `x` rounded to `n` decimals |
| `len(x)` | how many characters or items |
| `min(...)` `max(...)` `sum(...)` | smallest, largest, total |


# Methods: a function with a home

A **method** belongs to a value and is called with a **dot**:

```{pyodide}
ticker = "aapl"
ticker.upper()
```

The pattern is `value.method(arguments)`.


# One pattern, the whole course

That same dot-grammar runs everything ahead:

```python
ticker.upper()      # today: text
prices.sort()       # today: lists
returns.mean()      # Session 3: data tables
```

Different objects, identical shape. Learn `value.method(...)` once, and later
tools feel familiar. Try it live:

```{pyodide}
"Machine Learning in Finance".count("in")
```


# Your turn [3]{.chip .chip-turn} {.your-turn}

Build exactly this text with an f-string: `Total return: 35.56%`

```{pyodide}
#| autorun: false
jan = 183.56
dec = 248.83

total_return = ...
message = ...
message
```

::: {.muted .small}
First the return `(dec - jan) / jan`, then wrap it: `f"Total return: {…:.2%}"`.
:::


# 7 · Lists {.divider}

::: {.divider-kicker}
Part 7
:::

::: {.divider-note}
The workhorse of the day, and the foundation for the data tables that come later.
:::


# One year is 252 numbers

Apple's closing price, first week of 2024, in a single **list**:

```{pyodide}
closes = [183.56, 182.19, 179.87, 179.15, 183.48]
len(closes)
```

A list collects values, in order, under one name.


# Why lists matter

One variable per day would be absurd — a year has about **252 trading days**.
A list holds them all together.

::: {.keypoint}
Later in the course a data table will hold ten years of eleven stocks at once.
It is built directly on this idea: many values, in order, under one name.
:::


# Indexing: pick one day

Positions count **from zero** — the index is the distance from the start.

::: {.diagram}
<svg width="900" height="200" viewBox="0 0 900 200" role="img" aria-label="A list of five prices with indices 0 to 4 above and -5 to -1 below">
  <text x="55" y="50" font-size="18" fill="#1c5cab" font-weight="600">index</text>
  <text x="45" y="182" font-size="18" fill="#898781">from the end</text>
  <g font-family="Cascadia Mono, monospace">
    <text x="230" y="50" text-anchor="middle" font-size="21" fill="#1c5cab">0</text>
    <text x="370" y="50" text-anchor="middle" font-size="21" fill="#1c5cab">1</text>
    <text x="510" y="50" text-anchor="middle" font-size="21" fill="#1c5cab">2</text>
    <text x="650" y="50" text-anchor="middle" font-size="21" fill="#1c5cab">3</text>
    <text x="790" y="50" text-anchor="middle" font-size="21" fill="#1c5cab">4</text>
    <rect x="168" y="66" width="126" height="58" rx="4" fill="#ffffff" stroke="#c3c2b7" stroke-width="1.5"/>
    <rect x="308" y="66" width="126" height="58" rx="4" fill="#ffffff" stroke="#c3c2b7" stroke-width="1.5"/>
    <rect x="448" y="66" width="126" height="58" rx="4" fill="#ffffff" stroke="#c3c2b7" stroke-width="1.5"/>
    <rect x="588" y="66" width="126" height="58" rx="4" fill="#ffffff" stroke="#c3c2b7" stroke-width="1.5"/>
    <rect x="728" y="66" width="126" height="58" rx="4" fill="#ffffff" stroke="#c3c2b7" stroke-width="1.5"/>
    <text x="231" y="103" text-anchor="middle" font-size="20" fill="#141413">183.56</text>
    <text x="371" y="103" text-anchor="middle" font-size="20" fill="#141413">182.19</text>
    <text x="511" y="103" text-anchor="middle" font-size="20" fill="#141413">179.87</text>
    <text x="651" y="103" text-anchor="middle" font-size="20" fill="#141413">179.15</text>
    <text x="791" y="103" text-anchor="middle" font-size="20" fill="#141413">183.48</text>
    <text x="231" y="172" text-anchor="middle" font-size="20" fill="#898781">-5</text>
    <text x="371" y="172" text-anchor="middle" font-size="20" fill="#898781">-4</text>
    <text x="511" y="172" text-anchor="middle" font-size="20" fill="#898781">-3</text>
    <text x="651" y="172" text-anchor="middle" font-size="20" fill="#898781">-2</text>
    <text x="791" y="172" text-anchor="middle" font-size="20" fill="#898781">-1</text>
  </g>
</svg>
:::


# Indexing in action

```{pyodide}
closes = [183.56, 182.19, 179.87, 179.15, 183.48]
print(closes[0])
print(closes[2])
```

`[0]` is the first trading day, `[2]` the third. Change the index and run.


# Counting from the end

`[-1]` is the last item — whatever the list's length:

```{pyodide}
closes = [183.56, 182.19, 179.87, 179.15, 183.48]
closes[-1]
```

::: {.muted .small}
`[-1]` is worth loving. You rarely know a list's exact length; you always know
you want the most recent value.
:::


# Off the end [predict]{.chip .chip-predict}

Five prices sit at positions 0 to 4. **What will asking for `closes[5]` give
you?** Commit to an answer, then run.

```{pyodide}
#| autorun: false
closes = [183.56, 182.19, 179.87, 179.15, 183.48]
closes[5]
```

::: {.muted .small}
Positions run 0 to 4 — so what lives at position 5?
:::


# Slicing: pick a window

`list[start:stop]` — **start included, stop excluded.**

::: {.diagram}
<svg width="900" height="190" viewBox="0 0 900 190" role="img" aria-label="Slice 0 to 3 selects the first three cells; index 3 is excluded">
  <g font-family="Cascadia Mono, monospace">
    <text x="231" y="36" text-anchor="middle" font-size="20" fill="#1c5cab">0</text>
    <text x="371" y="36" text-anchor="middle" font-size="20" fill="#1c5cab">1</text>
    <text x="511" y="36" text-anchor="middle" font-size="20" fill="#1c5cab">2</text>
    <text x="651" y="36" text-anchor="middle" font-size="20" fill="#898781">3</text>
    <text x="791" y="36" text-anchor="middle" font-size="20" fill="#898781">4</text>
    <rect x="168" y="52" width="126" height="58" rx="4" fill="#dcebfd" stroke="#1c5cab" stroke-width="2"/>
    <rect x="308" y="52" width="126" height="58" rx="4" fill="#dcebfd" stroke="#1c5cab" stroke-width="2"/>
    <rect x="448" y="52" width="126" height="58" rx="4" fill="#dcebfd" stroke="#1c5cab" stroke-width="2"/>
    <rect x="588" y="52" width="126" height="58" rx="4" fill="#ffffff" stroke="#c3c2b7" stroke-width="1.5"/>
    <rect x="728" y="52" width="126" height="58" rx="4" fill="#ffffff" stroke="#c3c2b7" stroke-width="1.5"/>
    <text x="231" y="89" text-anchor="middle" font-size="20" fill="#141413">183.56</text>
    <text x="371" y="89" text-anchor="middle" font-size="20" fill="#141413">182.19</text>
    <text x="511" y="89" text-anchor="middle" font-size="20" fill="#141413">179.87</text>
    <text x="651" y="89" text-anchor="middle" font-size="20" fill="#898781">179.15</text>
    <text x="791" y="89" text-anchor="middle" font-size="20" fill="#898781">183.48</text>
  </g>
  <text x="450" y="152" text-anchor="middle" font-size="22" fill="#0d366b" font-family="Cascadia Mono, monospace">closes[0:3]</text>
  <text x="450" y="178" text-anchor="middle" font-size="16" fill="#898781">three items: 3 − 0 = 3 · the stop index is not included</text>
</svg>
:::


# Slicing in action

```{pyodide}
closes = [183.56, 182.19, 179.87, 179.15, 183.48]
closes[0:3]
```

Three items — positions 0, 1, 2. Position 3 is left out.


# Open-ended slices

Leave an end blank to mean "from the start" or "to the end":

```{pyodide}
closes = [183.56, 182.19, 179.87, 179.15, 183.48]
print(closes[:2])
print(closes[2:])
```


# Lists can grow

`.append(...)` adds a value to the end:

```{pyodide}
closes = [183.56, 182.19, 179.87, 179.15, 183.48]
closes.append(185.56)
closes
```

::: {.muted .small}
`.append` is a **method** — `closes.append(...)`, the dot-grammar again.
:::


# Where does a value sit?

`.index(...)` reports the **position** of a value:

```{pyodide}
closes = [183.56, 182.19, 179.87, 179.15, 183.48]
closes.index(179.15)
```

::: {.muted .small}
It found `179.15` at position 3. We will use this to locate a stock's worst
day.
:::


# Summaries over a whole list

The functions from earlier accept a list:

```{pyodide}
closes = [183.56, 182.19, 179.87, 179.15, 183.48]
print(min(closes), max(closes))
print(sum(closes) / len(closes))
```

::: {.muted .small}
`sum(...) / len(...)` is the average price of the week — your first statistic,
from raw parts.
:::


# Your turn [4]{.chip .chip-turn} {.your-turn}

From ten days, compute the **range** (highest minus lowest) of the **last
five** days only.

```{pyodide}
#| autorun: false
closes = [92.10, 93.45, 91.80, 94.20, 95.10,
          96.30, 94.85, 97.60, 96.90, 98.25]

last_five = ...
day_range = ...
day_range
```

::: {.muted .small}
Slice the last five first, then `max(...) - min(...)`. The answer is 3.40.
:::


# 8 · Errors are information {.divider}

::: {.divider-kicker}
Part 8
:::

::: {.divider-note}
An error message is a diagnosis, not a scolding.
:::


# An error is a diagnosis

Run this deliberately broken cell and read what comes back:

```{pyodide}
prices = [183.56, 182.19, 179.87]
prices[10]
```

Nothing is damaged. Your other variables are fine. The runtime is fine.


# Read it bottom-up

In Colab you get the **full traceback**. Read it from the bottom:

```text
IndexError                          Traceback (most recent call last)
      1 prices = [183.56, 182.19, 179.87]
----> 2 prices[10]

IndexError: list index out of range
```

- the **last line** names the error type and what went wrong
- the arrow **`---->`** marks the exact line that failed

::: {.keypoint}
Read the last line, look at the arrow, fix that line, run again. That resolves
most errors before you search for anything.
:::


# The four errors of week one

| error | usually means | first thing to check |
|---|---|---|
| `NameError` | name never assigned | typo? case? did that cell run? |
| `TypeError` | operation meets the wrong type | is a "number" secretly text? |
| `SyntaxError` | Python cannot parse the line | missing quote, bracket, or comma |
| `IndexError` | that position does not exist | off by one? empty list? |


# Error playground [optional]{.chip .chip-optional} {.your-turn}

Each line has one classic mistake. Remove one `#` at a time, run, and read the
message before fixing it.

```{pyodide}
#| autorun: false
price = 100.5
ticker = "AAPL"
week = [1.2, -0.4, 0.8]

# pirce + 1
# ticker + price
# week[3]
```

::: {.muted .small}
Which of the four error types does each one produce?
:::


# 9 · A week of Apple, by hand {.divider}

::: {.divider-kicker}
Part 9
:::

::: {.divider-note}
Everything from today, on real market data — and the course case begins.
:::


# The simple return

Yesterday's close $p_0$, today's close $p_1$. The **simple return** is

$$ r = \frac{p_1 - p_0}{p_0} $$

the percentage change in price — what one unit invested earned in a day. It is
the basic object of this whole course.

::: {.muted .small}
Deliberately familiar: the same percentage change you know from econometrics
growth rates.
:::


# Apple's first week of 2024

Two lists, kept in step — a date and a price at each position:

```{pyodide}
dates  = ["Jan 02", "Jan 03", "Jan 04", "Jan 05", "Jan 08"]
closes = [183.56, 182.19, 179.87, 179.15, 183.48]
closes[0]
```


# One day's return

From the first close to the second:

```{pyodide}
closes = [183.56, 182.19, 179.87, 179.15, 183.48]
(closes[1] - closes[0]) / closes[0]
```

::: {.muted .small}
Apple lost about three-quarters of a percent on the first trading day of 2024.
:::


# Format it properly

The same return, as a clean percentage with the day attached:

```{pyodide}
dates  = ["Jan 02", "Jan 03", "Jan 04", "Jan 05", "Jan 08"]
closes = [183.56, 182.19, 179.87, 179.15, 183.48]
r1 = (closes[1] - closes[0]) / closes[0]
f"Return on {dates[1]}: {r1:.2%}"
```


# The whole week, combined

Return over the week, plus the lowest day — using indexing, `min`, and
`.index` together:

```{pyodide}
dates  = ["Jan 02", "Jan 03", "Jan 04", "Jan 05", "Jan 08"]
closes = [183.56, 182.19, 179.87, 179.15, 183.48]
week_return = (closes[-1] - closes[0]) / closes[0]
lowest = min(closes)
low_day = dates[closes.index(lowest)]
f"Week: {week_return:.2%}, low of {lowest} on {low_day}"
```

::: {.muted .small}
`closes.index(lowest)` finds *where* the minimum sits; `dates[...]` reads the
same position in the other list.
:::


# Two lists, kept in step

::: {.keypoint}
Dates in one list, prices in another, connected only by position. Next week a
data **table** keeps them in one object, so they can never drift apart.
:::

That single idea — many aligned columns in one structure — is what makes
pandas, and everything after it, possible.


# The year that week came from

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| fig-align: center
from pathlib import Path
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

plt.rcParams.update({
    "figure.facecolor": "#fcfcfb", "axes.facecolor": "#fcfcfb",
    "savefig.facecolor": "#fcfcfb", "font.family": ["Segoe UI", "Arial", "sans-serif"],
    "font.size": 12, "text.color": "#141413", "axes.edgecolor": "#c3c2b7",
    "axes.labelcolor": "#52514e", "axes.titlecolor": "#141413", "axes.titlesize": 13,
    "axes.spines.top": False, "axes.spines.right": False, "axes.grid": True,
    "grid.color": "#e6e5df", "grid.linewidth": 0.8, "xtick.color": "#898781",
    "ytick.color": "#898781", "xtick.labelsize": 10.5, "ytick.labelsize": 10.5,
})

data_file = Path("../data/aapl_2024_closes.csv")
if not data_file.exists():
    raise FileNotFoundError(
        "Missing ../data/aapl_2024_closes.csv - clone the full course "
        "repository, or run tools/build_dataset.py from the repository root."
    )
aapl = pd.read_csv(data_file, parse_dates=["date"])

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9.6, 3.5))
ax.plot(aapl["date"], aapl["close"], color="#2a78d6", linewidth=2)
ax.axvspan(pd.Timestamp("2024-01-02"), pd.Timestamp("2024-01-08"),
           color="#cde2fb", alpha=0.9, zorder=0)
ax.set_ylim(155, 275)
ax.annotate("the week you just analysed",
            xy=(pd.Timestamp("2024-01-06"), 184),
            xytext=(pd.Timestamp("2024-01-22"), 250),
            fontsize=10.5, color="#52514e",
            arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="-", color="#898781", linewidth=1))
ax.set_title("Apple (AAPL) daily closing price, 2024", loc="left")
ax.set_ylabel("US dollars")
ax.margins(x=0.01)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

252 trading days; our five are the shaded sliver. Doing a whole year by hand
would be painful, which is why Session 3 brings in **pandas**.


# The course case [case]{.chip .chip-case}

**The Analyst's Notebook** — one investigation, four chapters, done at home.

You are a junior analyst building a **risk report** on a small universe of US
stocks. The running question:

::: {.keypoint}
How much do these stocks move, how does that differ across them — and could
that movement be **predicted** at all?
:::

By the end of these four sessions you will have framed that question
precisely, with real data. The actual model-building comes in the weeks after.


# The case: four chapters

| | data | your tools |
|---|---|---|
| Part 1 · today | Apple & Coca-Cola, 2024 daily closes | lists, indexing, returns |
| Part 2 | the same, measured properly | functions, loops, dictionaries |
| Part 3 | 11 stocks, 2015–2024 | pandas tables and plots |
| Part 4 | the same, as a prediction problem | features, targets, evaluation |

::: {.muted .small}
No live downloads: every price ships with the course. Results are identical
for everyone. Descriptive analysis first; the language of prediction comes in
carefully, later.
:::


# Case Part 1: this week

`session_01_case.ipynb`, about 10 questions — done at home, before Session 2:

- load Apple's 252 closing prices of 2024 *(loader provided)*
- first close, last close, best day, worst day
- slice January and measure its return
- one broken cell to diagnose and fix
- the finish: the same measurements for Coca-Cola, and a first judgment —
  **which of the two moved more in 2024?**

::: {.muted .small}
Hints and full solutions are folded into the notebook. Work first, then check.
:::


# What stays with you {.smaller}

::: {.recap-grid}
::: {.recap-item}
**Runtime state**
Cells share a memory; "correct" means correct top-to-bottom from a fresh start.
:::
::: {.recap-item}
**Variables**
`=` assigns a name to a value; assigning again overwrites.
:::
::: {.recap-item}
**Types**
`int`, `float`, `str`, `bool` — and `"150"` is not 150.
:::
::: {.recap-item}
**Formatting**
`f"{r:.2%}"` and `f"{p:.2f}"` — the two recipes we reuse.
:::
::: {.recap-item}
**Functions & methods**
`round(x, 2)` versus `ticker.upper()` — the dot-grammar runs the course.
:::
::: {.recap-item}
**Lists**
Index from 0, `[-1]` is the end, slice `[a:b]` excludes `b`.
:::
::: {.recap-item}
**Errors**
Read the last line first. Nothing is broken.
:::
::: {.recap-item}
**Simple return**
`(p1 - p0) / p0` — the object this course is about.
:::
:::


# Quick check [optional]{.chip .chip-optional} {.smaller}

Without running anything — what does each line produce? *(a value, or which
error?)*

```python
a = [10, 20, 30]

a[3]                # 1
"5" + "5"           # 2
a[-1] > 25          # 3
share_count         # 4    (never assigned)
a[1:3]              # 5
```

::: {.muted .small}
Answers on the next slide — argue with your neighbour first.
:::


# Quick check — answers {.smaller}

| | expression | result |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | `a[3]` | `IndexError` — positions are 0, 1, 2 |
| 2 | `"5" + "5"` | `"55"` — strings glue |
| 3 | `a[-1] > 25` | `True` — 30 > 25 |
| 4 | `share_count` | `NameError` — never assigned |
| 5 | `a[1:3]` | `[20, 30]` — stop excluded |


# Before Session 2

<div class="pillars">
<div class="pillar"><div class="pillar-tag">Homework</div><div class="pillar-title">Exercises</div><div class="pillar-body"><code>session_01_exercises.ipynb</code> — about 50 short tasks. Hints and solutions folded in.</div></div>
<div class="pillar"><div class="pillar-tag">Homework</div><div class="pillar-title">Case Part 1</div><div class="pillar-body"><code>session_01_case.ipynb</code> — the Apple versus Coca-Cola report.</div></div>
<div class="pillar"><div class="pillar-tag">If stuck</div><div class="pillar-title">Get help</div><div class="pillar-body">Ask a friend, ask an AI for a hint, or email me at <code>jobo@econ.au.dk</code>. Stuck over 15 minutes? Note it and bring it next session.</div></div>
</div>

::: {.muted .small}
**Next session:** functions, loops, and dictionaries, the tools that let you
repeat a calculation over hundreds of prices at once instead of by hand.
:::


# Live: getting set up {.divider}

::: {.divider-kicker}
Now, together
:::

::: {.divider-note}
Opening Google Colab, saving your own copy, and running your first cells — live.
:::
